qa_pairs.question: What is SSH and what is its purpose?
qa_pairs.answer: SSH is the software protocol used to connect to our clusters. It protects the security of your communication by verifying the server’s identity and yours against known identification data, and by encrypting the connection.

qa_pairs.question: Why are changes being made to the SSH protocol?
qa_pairs.answer: Because security risks evolve over time, support for certain SSH options that are no longer deemed secure will be ended.

qa_pairs.question: What actions do users need to take to continue using the clusters after the SSH changes?
qa_pairs.answer: Users will have to make some changes on their part in order to continue using the clusters.

qa_pairs.question: When were emails sent to users explaining the SSH changes?
qa_pairs.answer: An email with "IMPORTANT" in the subject line was sent to all users on July 29, and with more detail on September 16.

qa_pairs.question: What is the primary reason for modifying policies and practices regarding SSH?
qa_pairs.answer: With the constant increase in computing power, some encryption algorithms and protocols that were reasonably secure ten or fifteen years ago now pose an unacceptable risk of the connection being compromised by a third party.

qa_pairs.question: What might users need to update or generate due to the SSH policy changes?
qa_pairs.answer: Some users may have to update their SSH client software, some may have to generate a new public/private key-pair, and everyone will have to update their local copy of the "host key".

qa_pairs.question: When were the SSH security improvements implemented on the clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: The SSH security improvements were implemented on September 24, 2019, on Graham, and one week later on Béluga and Cedar.

qa_pairs.question: What are the three main SSH security improvements that have been implemented?
qa_pairs.answer: The implemented improvements are: disabling certain encryption algorithms, disabling certain public key types, and regenerating the cluster's host keys.

qa_pairs.question: What should users do if they don't understand terms like "encryption algorithms," "public keys," or "host keys"?
qa_pairs.answer: If users do not understand the significance of these terms, they should simply follow the steps outlined in the document.

qa_pairs.question: Which cluster is not affected by the upcoming SSH changes and why?
qa_pairs.answer: The upcoming changes do not concern Arbutus because users do not connect to it via SSH but through a web interface.

qa_pairs.question: Were there any prior SSH updates on other clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, there were earlier, less comprehensive updates made to Niagara on May 31, 2019, and to Graham in early August.

qa_pairs.question: What type of warning message might users see the first time they log in to a cluster after the changes?
qa_pairs.answer: Users will probably see a warning message like "WARNING: REMOTE HOST IDENTIFICATION HAS CHANGED!"

qa_pairs.question: Why does the SSH client display a "REMOTE HOST IDENTIFICATION HAS CHANGED!" warning?
qa_pairs.answer: This warning is displayed because the host keys on the cluster (e.g., Graham) were changed, and the user's SSH client software remembers the old host keys to prevent "man-in-the-middle" attacks.

qa_pairs.question: How many times might a user see the "REMOTE HOST IDENTIFICATION HAS CHANGED!" warning?
qa_pairs.answer: This warning will happen for your SSH client on each device from which you connect, so you may see it multiple times.

qa_pairs.question: What other related warning might users receive besides the host identification change?
qa_pairs.answer: Users may also get a warning regarding "DNS spoofing," which is related to the same change.

qa_pairs.question: How should users of MobaXterm, PuTTY, or WinSCP handle the host key warning on Windows?
qa_pairs.answer: The warning will appear in a pop-up window and will allow users to accept the new host key by clicking "Yes", but only if the fingerprint matches one listed in the SSH host key fingerprints section.

qa_pairs.question: What action should be taken if the fingerprint in the warning does not match the listed SSH host key fingerprints when using MobaXterm, PuTTY, or WinSCP?
qa_pairs.answer: If the fingerprint does not match any on the list, users should not accept the connection and contact Technical Support with the details.
